coredata.dc:title: Highly luminescent nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots sensitized TiO2 nanorod arrays for enhanced photoelectrochemical performance
coredata.dc:creator: Debnath, Kamalesh
Majumder, Tanmoy
Mondal, Suvra Prakash
coredata.dc:description: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) properties of N doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) sensitized TiO2 nanorods (NRs) have been studied. NGQDs have been prepared by using a facile and low temperature solvothermal process. TiO2 NRs have been grown on fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) coated glass substrate using hydrothermal method. The microstructure, chemical bonding and optical properties of the NGQDs have been investigated in details. The NGQDs are highly crystalline with an average diameter of ~8 nm and demonstrate excellent excitation dependent photoluminescence (PL). The PEC properties of TiO2 NRAs have been studied after attachment of NGQDs at different loading times and optimum performance has been obtained at one hour. The photoresponse and PEC performance of TiO2 NRs have been boosted significantly after the attachment of NGQDs. The photoconversion efficiency (η%) and incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) of NGQDs sensitized TiO2 NRs has been improved by 85.3% and 89.4%, respectively compared to pristine TiO2 NRs. The charge transfer resistance (Rct ) at nanorods/electrolyte junction has been decreased by 56.5% compared to pristine nanorods under white light illumination. In this study, the role of NGQDs as a green photosensitizer with TiO2 NRAs has been explored in detail.

head.ce:title: Highly luminescent nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots sensitized TiO2 nanorod arrays for enhanced photoelectrochemical performance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photoelectrochemical (PEC) properties of N doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) sensitized TiO2 nanorods (NRs) have been studied. NGQDs have been prepared by using a facile and low temperature solvothermal process. TiO2 NRs have been grown on fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) coated glass substrate using hydrothermal method. The microstructure, chemical bonding and optical properties of the NGQDs have been investigated in details. The NGQDs are highly crystalline with an average diameter of ~8 nm and demonstrate excellent excitation dependent photoluminescence (PL). The PEC properties of TiO2 NRAs have been studied after attachment of NGQDs at different loading times and optimum performance has been obtained at one hour. The photoresponse and PEC performance of TiO2 NRs have been boosted significantly after the attachment of NGQDs. The photoconversion efficiency (η%) and incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) of NGQDs sensitized TiO2 NRs has been improved by 85.3% and 89.4%, respectively compared to pristine TiO2 NRs. The charge transfer resistance (Rct ) at nanorods/electrolyte junction has been decreased by 56.5% compared to pristine nanorods under white light illumination. In this study, the role of NGQDs as a green photosensitizer with TiO2 NRAs has been explored in detail.

ce:section.ce:para: The pioneering study on water splitting by Fujishima and Honda in 1972 [1] had triggered a tremendous impetus in the investigation of photoelectrochemical (PEC) cells [2–4]. Over the last few decades, many semiconducting materials like ZnO [5,6], TiO2 [7], Fe2O3 [8], WO3 [9], BiVO4 [9], SrTiO3 [10], Cu2O [11], CdS [5], Ta3N5 [12], C3N4 [13], etc., were extensively explored as working electrode in PEC applications. Among all the photoanodes, TiO2 prevailed as the most promising material because of its earth-abundance, facile synthesis route, less photocorrosion, low cost, non-toxicity, and suitable band-edge positions for water splitting [14]. In particular, one-dimensional (1D) single-crystalline TiO2 nanorods have attracted major research interest due to their higher surface area, which results in better light-harvesting and increased active sites for electrode/electrolyte interfacial interaction. Such 1D nanostructures also promote improved charge separation and efficient charge transport to the collecting electrodes through direct and ordered pathways of their crystalline channels [15]. However, TiO2 NR based photoanodes suffer from low solar energy conversion efficiency due to their weak visible light absorption (bandgap ∼ 3.0–3.2 eV) and high intrinsic recombination rate of photo-generated electron-hole pairs [16].

Several strategies explored by researchers to improve visible light absorption includes heterostructures [17], morphology control [18,19], doping with metallic (Cu, Sn, Fe, etc.) [20,21] and non-metallic (N, F, C, S, B, etc.) [22–24] atoms, noble metal (Au, Ag, Pt, Pd) sensitization [5,25], and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [26]. The use of narrow bandgap metal chalcogenide materials like CdS, CdSe, PbS, PbSe, etc., boost the PEC performance of TiO2 NRs due to their size-tunable photoabsorption in a broad spectral range, multiple exciton generation and formation of heterojunction with favourable band alignment. However, these metal chalcogenide materials suffer from bottlenecks of high photocorrosion rate in the liquid medium and environmental concerns related to their toxic nature [27].

Recently graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been widely studied as a photosensitizer in PEC applications to substitute the inorganic semiconductor QDs [28,29]. GQDs are zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterial comprising a few layers of graphene sheets with smaller dimensions (below 10 nm) [30]. These 0D nanomaterials, apart from the size-tunable optical and electrical properties akin to the inorganic semiconductors, also offers attractive features of cost-effectiveness, facile synthesis process, eco-friendly nature, excellent biocompatibility and high stability [31]. GQDs were studied as a potential green material in various applications such as light-emitting devices [32], photocatalyst [33], supercapacitor [34], bio-imaging [35], drug delivery [36], resistive switching [37], photovoltaics [38] and photoelectrochemical devices [39,40]. GQD sensitized TiO2 nanorods have attracted much attention in photoelectrochemical solar energy conversion due to their matched band alignment, fast photo-generated charge separation, higher carrier lifetime and significantly low recombination loss [41]. The in-plane doping of heteroatoms like N [42], S [43], B [44], P [45], etc., in GQDs is an effective method to adjust the band structures, tune the optical properties and improve the activities towards photodriven reactions. T. Zhou et al. reported an improvement in the PEC performance of carbon quantum dot modified electrochemically reduced TiO2 (CQDs/E-TiO2) photoanodes, attributed to the synergistic effect of electrochemically induced oxygen vacancies (OV) in TiO2 and the OV-rich CQDs [46]. Xie et al. reported that the photocatalytic ability of S, N-GQD/TiO2 (P25) composite was 3.6 times higher than pure P25 [47]. Kai-An Tsai et al. reported a significant photoactivity in the visible region due to the introduction of CN bonds, which enhanced the visible absorption of N-GQDs [48]. Hui Luo et al. reported a higher photocurrent in the nitrogen doped carbon dots sensitized TiO2 (NCD/TiO2) electrode and an enhanced visible light absorption due to the formation of a type II heterojunction at the NCD/TiO2 interface [49]. Chan-Wei Hsu et al. reported an 86% photocurrent boost at 1.23 V (Vs RHE) under simulated sunlight illumination in the type II heterostructure photoanode prepared from rutile TiO2 nanorod coated with N-doped carbon layer containing a dispersion of N-doped carbon dots [50]. Qun Wang et al. used silk fibroin-derived nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) to form heterostructure with TiO2 nanotube and demonstrated improved photocatalytic performance [51].

In this report, we have demonstrated superior PEC properties of TiO2 NRs by sensitizing them with NGQDs. Highly luminescent NGQDs were synthesized using a simple one-step solvothermal process, using citric acid as carbon and urea as nitrogen source. Vertically aligned TiO2 NRs were grown using a hydrothermal method on an FTO substrate. The photoelectrochemical properties of NGQD sensitized TiO2 nanorods at different NGQD loading times were studied to evaluate the performance of an optimized photoanode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth of TiO2 nanorod (TiO2 NR) arrays
ce:section.ce:para: TiO2 NR arrays were grown on fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) coated glass substrate using the well-established hydrothermal procedure [52]. In a typical experiment, 12 ml of concentrated Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%, ACS reagent, Merck, Germany) was added dropwise to 18 ml of de-ionized water (DI, 18.2 MW.cm, Milli-Q direct 8 system) in a beaker. Subsequently, 0.4 ml of titanium (IV) butoxide (Reagent grade, 97%, Sigma Aldrich) was added to the prepared solution and stirred for 10–15 mins, resulting in a clear transparent precursor solution. FTO coated glass substrate (sheet resistance 7 Ω per square, Sigma Aldrich) of dimension 2.5 × 1 cm2 was washed with a neutral laboratory detergent (Vetroclean, Borosil, India) to remove dirt and grease. The washed FTO substrate was then cleaned ultrasonically in a solution containing DI water, acetone (analytical reagent, ≥ 99.5%, Rankem, India) and 2-Propanol (ACS reagent, 99.8%, Merck, Germany), mixed in a volume ratio of 1:1:1. The cleaned and dried FTO substrate with the conducting side facing down was dipped in the precursor solution, poured inside a 25 ml Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was sealed and placed in an oven for hydrothermal growth at a temperature of 180 °C for 6 hrs. After completion of the growth process, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, resulting in a visibly uniform TiO2 film on the conducting side of the FTO substrate. All samples were washed thoroughly with DI water and annealed at 500 °C for 3 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) and attachment with TiO2 NRs
ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen-doped Graphene Quantum dots (NGQDs) were synthesized by a solvothermal technique using a solution of citric acid and urea (molar ratio of 1:3) dissolved in N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, ≥ 99.8, ACS reagent, Merck, Germany). In a typical experiment, 1.26 gm (6 mmol) of citric acid (purity ≥ 99.5%, ACS reagent, Merck, Germany) and 1.081 gm (18 mmol) of urea (purity: 99 – 100.5%, ACS reagent, Merck, Germany) was added to 20 ml of DMF solution and stirred magnetically for about 30 mins, resulting in a clear transparent solution. The solution was transferred to a 50 ml Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave placed in an electric oven at a temperature of 180 °C for 8 h. After cooling down to room temperature, a reddish-brown solution was obtained, which confirmed the formation of NGQDs. The above solution was mixed with a 30 ml solution of ethanol (purity ≥ 99.9%, ACS reagent, Merck, Germany) and DI water (volume ratio of 3:1) and centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 mins to collect the NGQDs as a precipitate. The precipitate was then dispersed in 30 ml of DI water and stored for further use. The NGQDs were attached to the surface of TiO2 NRs by dipping in the aqueous solution of NGQD for 30mins to 5hrs. The NGQD sensitized TiO2 nanorod (NGQD/TiO2 NR) photoanodes were thoroughly washed in DI water and dried on a hot plate at 120 °C for 2 hrs.

The crystalline nature of the samples was examined by using a Bruker D8 Advanced x-ray diffractometer with a
C u
K α
(λ = 1.54056 Å) source. The scanning electron micrographs of TiO2 NR arrays were studied using a JEOL JSM-7600F electron microscope. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) study of NGQDs and TiO2 NRs were carried out using a JEOL, JEM-2100Plus electron microscope. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) measurements of NGQD sample was carried out using a Spectrum II, PerkinElmer system. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using a PHI 5000 VersaProbe III system. Optical absorption spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu, UV-3600 Plus, ultraviolet–visible-infrared (UV–Vis-IR) spectrophotometer. Excitation dependent photoluminescence (PL) measurements of NGQDs were carried out using a Perkin Elmer spectrofluorometer (Model LS-55). PEC measurements of the photoanodes were recorded by employing an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660D, USA). A quartz beaker was used as a PEC cell comprising a conventional 3-electrode system with 1 M KOH aqueous solution (pH ∼ 13.6) as an electrolyte. The photoanode samples on FTO substrate (TiO2 NR arrays, NGQD/TiO2 NR arrays), saturated Ag/AgCl electrode (3.5 M KCl) and a platinum (Pt) wire were used as the working, reference and counter electrode, respectively. Photocurrent measurements were performed using a broadband light source of intensity ∼ 100 mW/cm2 (Sciencetech Canada). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) investigations were performed in the frequency range of 1 Hz to 100 kHz with an AC signal amplitude of ∼ 10 mV at a bias voltage of 0 V vs Ag/AgCl under dark and illumination of light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructural, chemical bonding and optical properties of NGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The plane view TEM image of the NGQDs is displayed in Fig. 1 (a) and shows that the particles are densely dispersed with a non-uniform morphology, although a high resolution image in Fig. 1(b) revealed isolated particles. The corresponding Gaussian fitted histogram of the NGQDs, presented at the inset of Fig. 1(a), shows a non-uniform lateral size distribution spanning over a range of 4 to 12 nm with an average size of 8.09 ± 0.28 nm. Three different NGQD samples are marked with yellow dashed squares and identified as (i), (ii), and (iii) in the HRTEM micrograph displayed in Fig. 1(b). The auto-correlated HRTEM lattice image of these samples and the corresponding line profile analysis of their lattice diffraction fringes are shown in Fig. 1(i-iii), whereas the inset displays the respective fast Fourier transform (FFT) patterns. The interplanar spacing of 0.243 nm and 0.241 nm in Fig. 1(i) and Fig. 1(ii), respectively correspond to the (1120) lattice plane of the graphene sheet. On the other hand, the interlayer spacing of 0.328 nm in Fig. 1(iii) represents the (002) plane of the adjacent graphene sheets [53,54]. The TEM study implied that the core structure of the NGQDs identified in Fig. 1(b) strongly resembles that of graphite.

The powder x-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of NGQDs in Fig. 2 (a) shows diffraction peaks at an angle (2θ) ∼ 27.2° and 40.1°. The diffraction peak at 2θ ∼ 27.2° is indexed to reflections from the (002) plane of the stacking graphene layers with an interlayer spacing of 0.328 nm [55], corroborating our HRTEM results. The reduction of the inter-planner spacing of NGQDs compared to bulk graphite crystal results from the shrinkage of the crystal plane due to the higher electronegativity and smaller radius of N dopant element in the hexagonal carbon matrix [56]. The XRD peak at 2θ ∼ 40.1° is attributed to (100) plane (lattice spacing ∼ 0.216 nm) associated with the reflections from the honeycomb structure of the graphene layer [56,57] . A schematic of the NGQD structure, as revealed by the TEM and XRD studies, is presented in Fig. 2(b).

The chemical bonding and functional groups attached to NGQDs were studied using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Supporting Information, Fig. S1). Broad absorption peaks at 3425 cm−1 and 3195 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH, respectively, and indicate the presence of abundant hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of the NGQDs [58]. The peaks at 1706 cm−1 and 1662 cm−1 are attributed to CO and CN stretching vibration, respectively [59]. The peak at 1633 cm−1 and 1385 cm−1 is assigned to CC in-plane stretching vibrations of sp2 carbon atoms and bending vibrations of C-OH, respectively [60]. Absorption peaks at 1401 cm−1 and 1022 cm−1 are associated with the in-plane stretching vibration of CN bonds [61], whereas the peak at 1461 cm−1 is attached to the amide NH vibrations [62]. Further, the absorptions peaks between 1100 and 1260 cm−1 correspond to the COC/CO/CH vibrations [63]. The FTIR analysis indicates the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups like hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl and nitrogen-containing functional groups such as amino and amide on the surface of NGQDs.

The elemental composition, surface groups and chemical bonding in NGQDs were analysed by multiple peak-fitting of the high-resolution XPS spectrum using Gaussian function. XPS survey scan of the NGQDs in Fig. 3 (a) revealed three peaks at 284.6, 398.6 and 530.6 eV corresponding to carbon (C1s), nitrogen (N1s) and oxygen (O1s) elements, respectively [42]. The presence of the N1s peak in the full scan spectrum confirmed the successful incorporation of dopant N atoms in the sp2 carbon matrix of GQD [64]. The absence of any other peak implies a relatively contaminant-free NGQDs surface. The atomic concentration of 14.32% N indicates a high doping level, whereas the O/C atomic ratio of 35% suggests the strong presence of oxygen-containing functional groups. The High-resolution XPS spectrum of N 1s electron in Fig. 3(b) has been deconvoluted into three Gaussian components, each associated with CN bonding originating from the incorporation of N atoms in the carbon matrix. The deconvoluted peaks at 398.68, 399.93 and 401.9 eV represent the formation of pyridinic N (CN-C), pyrrolic N (CNC) and graphitic N (N-C3), respectively [65]. The relative intensities of the fitted peaks show that the pyrrolic N dominates over graphitic and pyridinic N components in our as grown NGQDs. The CN bonds enhance the visible absorption of the NGQDs, whereas the graphitic N increases the electrical conductivity of the NGQD sample [48].

The C1s XPS spectrum in Fig. 3(c) has been fitted into five peaks, indicating five types of carbon bonds. The highest intensity peak is the graphitic peak at the binding energy of 284.6 eV, indexed to CC/CC bonds and signified that most carbon atoms are sp2 hybridized, conforming to the hexagonal structure of graphene. The nitrogenated peak at 285.9 eV corresponds to sp3 carbon of CO/CN bonds and further confirms the successful doping of nitrogen [66]. The oxygenated peak at 287.0 eV, 288.2 eV and 289.3 eV corresponds to COC/C-OH (Hydroxyl group), CO (Carbonyl group) and O-CO (Carboxylate group), respectively and affirms the existence of O-rich functional groups [67]. The O 1 s XPS peak presented in Fig. 3(d) has been deconvoluted into two Gaussian components with the peak at 531.46 eV and 533.0 eV, attributed to CO and C-OH/COC bonds, respectively [68]. Fig. 3(e) shows the schematic diagram of various bonding of a typical NGQD obtained from our FTIR and XPS studies.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the NGQD in an aqueous solution is shown in Fig. 4 (a). The spectrum exhibits a weak absorption peak centred at 247 nm, assigned to the intrinsic (π − π*) transition of graphitic sp2 domains (CC). There are two absorption bands centred at 340 nm and 437 nm, with a broad tail extending beyond 650 nm in the visible region. The former is attributed to (n − π*) transition of the oxygeneous functional groups (CO/CO) at the edge of NGQDs, whereas the latter is associated with (n − π*) transition in CN/CN [54].

The room temperature photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the NGQDs at increasing excitation wavelengths is shown in Fig. 4(b). The normalized PL spectra in the inset of Fig. 4(b) depicts an excitation dependent emission from NGQDs. The PL peak redshifted from 443 nm to 560 nm under the excitation of light of wavelengths 340 nm to 500 nm. The excitation dependent fluorescence behaviour was visible to the bare eye. The photographic image of aqueous NGQDs under sunlight and different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. 4(c) (i) and (ii), respectively. The exact mechanism of PL emission in graphene and carbon quantum dots is still under investigation. However, as per the earlier reports, the PL in NGQDs may have resulted from the size-dependent quantum confinement effect, free zigzag sites and additional radiative recombination through multiple electron transition pathways [69,70]. The excitation wavelength-dependent emission of NGQDs may be due to electronic transitions from different sp2 related localized states originated by the non-uniform size distribution and the surface states introduced by nitrogen doping [71].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of TiO2 NR arrays and NGQD attached TiO2 NR arrays
ce:section.ce:para:
Top view SEM micrographs of TiO2 NR arrays grown on FTO substrate using a hydrothermal process is shown in Fig. 5 (a-c). Fig. 5(a) revealed a uniform growth of vertically aligned and densely packed nanorods with an average density of
2.21 ×
10
9
/
c m
2
. The magnified and cross-sectional SEM image of TiO2 NRs is presented in Fig. 5(b) and 5(c), respectively. The mean diameter of the nanorods is 169.96 ± 1.2 nm (Supporting Information, Fig. S2), whereas the length is ∼ 2 µm. The elemental analysis of nanorods performed using electron dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDX) revealed the presence of Ti and O elements, confirming the growth of TiO2 nanorods (Supporting Information, Fig. S3). Fig. 5(d) displays the typical XRD pattern of the pristine TiO2 NR arrays grown on the FTO coated glass substrate, annealed at 500 °C. The XRD peaks at (101), (111), (211) and (002) planes confirmed the formation of crystalline tetragonal rutile phase (JCPDS NO, 21–1276) with lattice parameters of a = b = 0.4593 nm and c = 0.2959 nm [72].

The UV–Visible absorption spectra of NGQD sensitized TiO2 NR (NGQD/TiO2 NR) samples is presented in Fig. 6 (a) and demonstrated an improved visible photo-absorption tail up to ∼ 700 nm, relative to pristine TiO2 NRs. The enhanced absorption indicated the successful attachment of NGQD on the TiO2 NRs. The absorption increases in the visible region with the increase in loading time of the NGQD (inset of Fig. 6(a)). The energy band gap
(
E g
)
of the samples was estimated using Tauc’s formula [46].

Where α is the absorption coefficient, hν is the incident photon energy, and A is a constant. The Tauc’s plots
i . e

of pristine TiO2 NRs and NGQD/TiO2 NRs at different loading times are presented in Fig. 6(b). The absorption edge of the pristine TiO2 NRs is at around 405 nm with an estimated optical band gap
(
E
g )
∼ 3.18 eV, attributed to the characteristic UV absorption of the rutile TiO2 NRs [73]. However, NGQD/TiO2 NRs does not manifest any band-gap narrowing, which suggests that the NGQDs are anchored to the surface of TiO2 without disrupting its crystal structure [25,40,49]. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of NGQD in Fig. 4(a) has an optical absorption in both the ultraviolet and visible regions. Thus, the absorption of the NGQD/TiO2 hybrid structure in the UV region (300 nm − 400 nm) is due to the combined effect of both TiO2 nanorods and the attached NGQD, whereas the enhancement of the visible absorption (400 nm −700 nm) is possible only due to the intrinsic absorption of attached NGQDs [74].

The full range XPS survey of TiO2 NRs and the NGQD/TiO2 NRs are presented in Fig. 7 . The XPS spectrum of TiO2 NRs revealed the existence of Ti, O and C elements. The N 1s peak in the XPS of NGQD/TiO2 NR appeared from the nitrogen in NGQD and confirmed the successful attachment of NGQDs on the surface of the TiO2 NRs [75]. The C 1s peak of the NGQD/TiO2 NR is slightly strong compared to that of the adventitious carbon in TiO2 NRs, due to the carbon of the attached NGQDs [49]. The O1s peak of NGQD/TiO2 NR is the contribution of both TiO2 and NGQDs. The inset of Fig. 7 shows the high-resolution XPS spectrum of the Ti 2p electrons. The Ti 2p core level peak has been split into two asymmetric peaks of
T i 2 p
3 / 2
and
T i 2 p
1 / 2
attributed to spin-orbital splitting and located at 458.6 eV and 464.45 eV, respectively [76]. No apparent shift in the binding energy of
T i 2 p
3 / 2
and
T i 2 p
1 / 2
peaks in the NGQD/TiO2 NRs implied that the NGQDs are anchored on the TiO2 surface without any disruption or incorporation into its lattice structure [49,76].

To further confirm the attachment of NGQDs, HRXPS peaks of N1s, C1s and O1s have been deconvoluted into different Gaussian components and discussed in Supporting Information (Fig. S4 (a-c)). Our XPS study confirmed the successful attachment of NGQDs on the TiO2 NR surfaces. The presence of the TiOC peak at 531.27 eV in the O1s spectrum of NGQD/TiO2 NRs suggest that the NGQDs were attached to the TiO2 NRs, possibly through oxygen [63,49]. The presence of the TiOC bonds, besides improving the coupling between the TiO2 NRs and NGQDs, also enhance the interfacial charge transfer [63].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoelectrochemical properties of NGQD attached TiO2 NRAs
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical measurements of pristine TiO2 NR and NGQD/TiO2 NR photoanodes (working electrode) were performed in a photoelectrochemical (PEC) cell to understand the role of the NGQDs as a photosensitizer. To determine the optimum loading of NGQDs on the surface of TiO2 NR, attachment times of 30 min, 1 h, 2 h, 3 h, and 5 h has been considered. Fig. 8 (a) shows the linear sweep voltammograms (LSV) of pristine TiO2 NRs and the NGQD/TiO2 NRs at various attachment times under broadband light illumination with an intensity of 100 mW/cm2. The LSV of the bare pristine TiO2 NRs, under dark conditions, is shown in dotted lines in the above figure. In the case of TiO2 NRs, a small dark current of 2.33 µA/cm2 at 0.5 V vs Ag/AgCl, was observed that indicated a negligible photoelectrochemical activity in the absence of light [77]. The value of current density (J) of the pristine sample under illumination is 1.68 mA cm−2 at 0.5 V vs Ag/AgCl. The increase in the current densities of all the samples under light illumination is due to the photo-generated electron-hole pair, the efficient separation of which at a higher positive bias is driven by the increased electric field [78]. The photocurrent saturates at higher potential due to the limited number of electron-hole pairs at a constant illumination [79]. The photocurrent density is dependent on the attachment time. The current density of the photoanodes for attachment times of 30 min, 1 h, 2 h, 3 h, and 5 h is 2.32, 2.78, 2.55, 2.14, and 1.85 mA/cm2 at a bias voltage of 0.5 V vs Ag/AgCl, respectively. More importantly, the photocurrent of NGQD/ TiO2 NRs reaches a maximum of 2.78 mA cm−2 at a loading time of 1 h, which is 1.65 times higher compared to pristine TiO2 NRs. The transient photoresponse (J-t) of photoanodes, at a bias of 0 V vs Ag/AgCl, under the illumination of broadband light (100 mW/cm2), has been shown in Fig. 8(b). All the samples demonstrated fast transient response under chopped illumination with ON and OFF durations of 30 sec. The maximum current density obtained is 2.53 mA/cm2 at an attachment time of 1 h which is 1.73 times higher than pristine TiO2 NRs. The short-circuit photocurrent of NGQD decorated TiO2 NRs decreases beyond an attachment time of 1 h due to the increase in thickness of the NGQD layer [80].

The photoconversion efficiency (η%) of the samples has been evaluated from the LSV curves by using the following equation [77].
(2)

where J is the photocurrent density,

is the applied potential versus reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE), and

is the incident intensity of illumination (100 mW/cm2). The photoconversion efficiency (η%) of the photoanodes, as a function of the applied bias potential (vs Ag/AgCl), is shown in Fig. 8(c). In consistence with LSV and amperometric i-t study, the photoconversion efficiency (η%) of the photoanodes increases with NGQD loading, up till an attachment time of 1 h and gradually decreases afterwards. The maximum efficiency achieved was 1.39% (at −0.47 V vs Ag/AgCl) at a loading time of 1 h. This value is 85.3% higher than pristine TiO2 NRs with a maximum efficiency of 0.75% (at −0.45 V vs Ag/AgCl).

was measured, under a constant illumination of broadband light (100 mW/cm2) for a time interval of 50 sec, after which the light was turned off abruptly followed by the recording of time-dependent decay of

for the next 400 secs. Under steady illumination, the OCVD plots of all the photoanodes in Fig. 8(d) shows a sharp increase in

, which subsequently saturates due to equilibrium achieved by the generation and recombination of electron-hole pairs. Once the illumination is interrupted, the

for all the photoanodes are presented in the inset of Fig. 8(d) and demonstrated that the NGQD decorated TiO2 NRs has a higher electron lifetime compared to pristine TiO2 NRs. The highest recombination lifetime has been observed at an attachment time of 1 h, which may be attributed to the efficient and fast charge carrier separation and improved charge transport kinetics achieved after the attachment of NGQDs. The decrease in
τ n
at attachment times beyond 1 h may be due to the aggregation of NGQD and formation of a thick layer on the surface of TiO2 NRs, which would lead to recombination of photoexcited carriers at the boundaries of the quantum dots [80].

To further investigate the role of NGQD for improving the PEC performances, incident photon to current conversion efficiency (IPCE) measurements were carried out at 0 V vs Ag/AgCl bias voltage using the following equation [18].
(4)

J λ

where
J λ
is the photocurrent density (mA/cm2), and
P λ
is the intensity of the incident light (mW/cm2) at wavelength, λ (nm). Fig. 9 displays the IPCE (%) plots of TiO2 NRs and NGQDs attached TiO2 NRs with the optimized loading time (1 h). The IPCE (%) of NGQD/TiO2 NRs has significantly increased compared to the bare pristine TiO2 NRs in the wavelength ranging from 300 nm to 460 nm. The NGQD/TiO2 NRs showed a maximum IPCE value of 53.4% at 340 nm relative to pristine TiO2 NRs having a maximum of 28.2% at 360 nm, with an overall improvement of 89.4%. The magnified plot of IPCE (%) of NR samples in the wavelength range of 400 nm − 700 nm is presented at the inset of Fig. 9. Interestingly, the hybrid structure recorded an improved IPCE compared to the pristine TiO2 NR in both the UV and the visible region, attributed to the photosensitizing role of NGQD. The improved IPCE of NGQD/TiO2 NRs is ascribed to the enhanced visible-photon harvesting, promoted by CN bond configurations in the NGQDs in synergy with the fast and efficient charge transfer in the thermodynamically favourable type II heterojunction at NGQD/TiO2 interface [48,49]. However, a weak visible photoabsorption and photocurrent have been observed in the pristine TiO2 NRs, which may be attributed to the surface defect states and light scattering inside the nanorod arrays [18,77].

The charge transfer process at the electrode/electrolyte interface was studied using EIS measurements, carried out in the frequency interval of 1 Hz to 100 kHz at 0 V vs Ag/AgCl under dark and illumination of broadband light intensity of 100 mW/cm2. Fig. 10 (a) represents the Nyquist plot of photoanodes, a plot of real [Re (Z′)] vs Imaginary [Im (Z″)] component of complex impedance [Z(ω) = Re (Z′) + j Im(Z″)], under dark and illumination condition. The upper inset in Fig. 10(a) displays the equivalent circuit model at the photoanode/electrolyte interface, whereas the lower inset presents the expanded view of the EIS plot under illumination. In the equivalent circuit model, RS is the series resistance, RSC is the semiconductor resistance, whereas Cdl is the depletion layer capacitance. On the other hand, Rct and CH denote the charge transfer resistance and Helmholtz double-layer capacitance at the semiconductor/electrolyte interface, respectively [29]. The estimated values of RS, RSC, Cdl, Rct, and CH, under dark and illumination conditions obtained from fitted results, are listed in Table S1 of the Supporting information. Under illumination, the value of Rct for TiO2 NRs and NGQD/TiO2 NRs changes from
7.5 ± 0.36 k Ω
c m
- 1
to
1.1 ± 0.031 k Ω
c m
- 1
and
5.1 ± 0.21 k Ω
c m
- 1
to
478 ± 19.23 Ω
c m
- 1
, respectively. The significant decrease in the Rct value of NGQDs decorated TiO2 NRs compared to pristine TiO2 NRs, is attributed to the enhanced charge transfer rate in the heterojunction at the semiconductor/electrolyte interface. Such an improved charge transfer efficiency enables fast carrier separation that lowers the recombination rate and enhances the number of effective photo-generated carriers [83]. The superior PEC performance of the NGQDs decorated TiO2 NRs is attributed to the strong photoabsorption of the NGQDs in the UV–visible region, efficient charge transfer kinetics, fast charge separation, lowered recombination rate, higher carrier lifetime and lowered interfacial resistance at the interface of the TiO2/NGQD and the electrolyte.

The stability of the NGQD/TiO2 NR photoanode sample was tested by measuring the photocurrent density under broadband light illumination (100 mW/cm2) at 0 V vs Ag/AgCl (Supplementary Information, Fig. S5). There was no apparent change in the photocurrent even after three consecutive run cycles of 3600 secs each, indicating superior long term photostability, repeatability and excellent PEC durability of the NGQDs attached to the TiO2 NRs. Such excellent stability of graphene-based quantum dots was also reported in earlier studies [81,84].

The charge transfer process at the NGQD/TiO2 NRs photoanode under illumination is depicted schematically in Fig. 10(b). The energy levels of conduction and valence band of TiO2 NRs, LUMO and HOMO levels of NGQDs and the work function of FTO have been adopted from earlier reported literature [85]. The band cascade formation (type II heterojunction) with thermodynamically favourable band alignment at the NGQD/TiO2 NR junction allows efficient transfer of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs in both NGQDs and TiO2 NRs. The electrons in the LUMO levels of NGQD move toward the FTO substrate, whereas the holes in the valence band of TiO2 move towards the HOMO levels of NGQDs, to oxidize water to oxygen at the electrode/electrolyte interface. The electrons move from the FTO substrate through the external circuit to reduce water to hydrogen at the counter electrode.

To summarize this current work, we have synthesized N doped GQDs with an average size of 8.09 ± 0.28 nm by employing a low cost and facile solvothermal process. Our as grown NGQDs had absorption peaks at 247 nm, 340 nm and 437 nm with a broad tail extended beyond 650 nm. NGQDs are highly luminescent and demonstrated an excellent excitation dependent PL emission. The PL peaks have been red shifted from 443 nm to 560 nm, at excitation wavelengths ranging from 340 nm to 500 nm. The various bonding, functional groups and presence of doped N in the GQDs, were determined using FTIR and XPS studies. XPS study also confirmed the existence of pyrrolic, pyridinic and graphitic N configurations in NGQDs, with pyrrolic N being the most dominant of all the CN configurations. TiO2 NR photoanodes were grown on FTO coated glass substrates using the hydrothermal method. TiO2 NRs were highly crystalline with an average diameter and length of 169.96 ± 1.2 nm and 2 μm, respectively. The PEC properties of NGQDs sensitized TiO2 NRs at different loading times were studied, with the optimum performance obtained at an attachment time of 1 h. The optimized photoanode demonstrated an IPCE of value ∼ 53.4% at 340 nm, an overall improvement of 89.4% relative to pristine TiO2 NRs. The maximum photoconversion efficiency (η%) achieved was 1.39% at −0.47 V vs Ag/AgCl, a value that is 85.3% higher than TiO2 NRs. The NGQDs sensitized TiO2 NRs showed a photocurrent density of ∼ 2.78 mA/cm2 (at 0.5 V vs Ag/AgCl) and short circuit current density (Jsc) of ∼ 2.53 mA/cm2, respectively under broadband illumination of 100 mW/cm2. The overall PEC performance of TiO2 NR photoanodes after sensitization with NGQDs is attributed to the enhanced UV–visible light absorption, favourable band alignment of type II heterostructure, higher carrier lifetime and lowered charge transfer resistance at the electrode/electrolyte interface. This work demonstrated the potential role of N doped GQDs as a green photosensitizer with TiO2 NRAs photoanodes for low cost, environment-friendly solar energy conversion applications.
Kamalesh Debnath: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Tanmoy Majumder: Data curation, Formal analysis. Suvra Prakash Mondal: Supervision.
